items.title: Byelection clock ticking as MPs officially resign. How does it impact Liberals' majority?
Global shares fall as tech stocks tumble
items.summary_auto: Futures for major U.S. indexes pointed down before the opening bell on Tuesday, while global markets mostly tumbled. Japan's Nikkei lost 3.6 per cent and South Korea's KOSPI lost 10 per cent, while European markets had somewhat smaller swings.

items.title: Israeli gunfire kills 2 in south Lebanon, Civil Defence says
items.summary_auto: Israeli gunfire killed two people in southern Lebanon on Tuesday, Lebanon's Civil Defence and state media said, the first reported fatalities resulting from Israeli fire in Lebanon in three days.

items.title: Frank talk on Ottawa police misconduct dominates 1st in-person board meeting in years
items.summary_auto: Gender-based misconduct and what to do about it dominated the discussion at the first in-person Ottawa police oversight board meeting in more than three years Monday night.

items.title: Several First Nations sign deal with Ottawa, Ontario to own part of a nuclear reactor
items.summary_auto:
Several Ontario First Nations are set to become owners of a new nuclear reactor after striking a commercial partnership with the 
federal and provincial governments.

items.title: Surrey, B.C., extortionist told police that Bishnoi contractor paid $3K for fiery attack
items.summary_auto: Three Indian nationals jailed earlier this month for their roles in a fiery attack on a South Surrey home in February were paid $1,000 each by a subcontractor for the Bishnoi gang, according to a record of their sentencing.

items.title: Drive-thru chatbots are clocking in at fast-food chains
French officials warn of drowning dangers amid extreme heat, with 40 dead since last week
items.summary_auto: Millions of people across Europe were exposed to extreme and exceptionally high temperatures on Tuesday, with 40 fatalities from drowning recorded in France in the past week as residents seek relief from the searing heat.

items.title: Is the U.K. ungovernable?
items.summary_auto: With Keir Starmer out, the U.K.’s had six different Prime Ministers resign in the last 10 years. Why? And what does it say about what it takes to govern the country?

items.title: Years without fluoridated water in N.B. show pattern of tooth decay experts warned about
items.summary_auto: After more than a decade without fluoridated water in Saint John, the dental community is seeing a surge in tooth decay among young people.

items.title: N.S. banned cellphones in classrooms 2 years ago. Here's how it's going
items.summary_auto: Nearly two years into its provincewide ban on cellphones in Nova Scotia public schools, the province's education minister says it's helping to reduce distraction and improve learning. Some students are following the rules, while others need more convincing.

items.title: Years without fluoridated water show pattern of tooth decay experts warned about
items.summary_auto: After more than a decade without fluoridated water in Saint John, the dental community is seeing a surge in tooth decay among young people.

items.title: Why the wait? Delays in disclosure of evidence repeatedly flagged in N.L. Jordan cases
items.summary_auto: An analysis of cases impacted by R. v. Jordan shows that one of the driving factors is the delay in handing over disclosure — evidence in the case — to the defence.

items.title: Ontario's freedom of information changes are law. Why experts say they could hurt the government
items.summary_auto: The reality of Premier Doug Ford’s changes to Ontario’s freedom of information system are beginning to take hold as rejections and denials make their way to requesters.

items.title: How the FIFA World Cup is bringing a pan-African vibe to life in Toronto
items.summary_auto: Three of Toronto's five World Cup 2026 group games feature African teams. It's giving African fans in the city a "once-in-a-lifetime" opportunity to watch some of their continent's top talents and vibe with the best elements of pan-African culture.

items.title: Are more police officers getting killed in the line of duty? Here's what the data tells us
items.summary_auto: An armed standoff at a hotel in Côte-des-Neiges left a police officer, a civilian and a suspect dead. The shooting marked the third time an officer in Canada was killed in less than two weeks, raising questions about whether violence against officers is increasing.

items.title: Teen charged with attempted murder of 8-year-old girl in Quadeville, Ont., to stand trial in 2027
items.summary_auto: The teenager charged last summer with the attempted murder of an eight-year-old girl in Quadeville, Ont., is scheduled to stand trial in early 2027.

items.title: Do you want AI with that? Chatbots could take your next order at the drive-thru
items.summary_auto: Across the United States, fast food chains are turning to AI to run their drive-thru counters - and Canada could be next. Companies say the technology now has higher accuracy than human employees - and some labour organizers worry it could displace fast food workers.

items.title: Ransom note sent days after Nancy Guthrie's abduction said she was dead, sources say
items.summary_auto: A ransom note sent days after the disappearance of Nancy Guthrie, the mother of NBC Today show host Savannah Guthrie, said the 84-year-old had died, CNN and other news organizations reported Monday, citing law enforcement sources.

items.title: Mayor sets record straight on Nanaimo cookie mispronunciation | The Moment
items.summary_auto: Nanaimo, B.C., Mayor Leonard Krog tells The National how to pronounce the city’s name after videos circulated on social media of people mispronouncing it when buying Crumbl’s new Nanaimo cookie.

items.title: 'Completely unacceptable': Alleged attack on Victoria imam draws condemnation
items.summary_auto: Officials in B.C. are condemning an alleged attack on the imam of a Victoria mosque last week.

items.title: Montreal police officer and civilian killed in Côte-des-Neiges shooting identified
items.summary_auto: Montreal police announced "with profound sadness" that Const. Mohamed Lamine Benredouane was the officer who was killed in the line of duty Monday during an intervention in the Côte-des-Neiges neighbourhood.

items.title: Did the Ontario Progressive Conservatives edit a Ford Fest photo? Here's what we found
items.summary_auto: The Ontario government’s Official Opposition slammed Premier Doug Ford Monday after a photograph shared online by two PC MPPs showcasing Ford Fest — an annual community event held by the party — appeared to be edited to make protesters look like supporters.

items.title: Ford, autoworkers' union kick off contract talks as CUSMA cloud hangs over sector
items.summary_auto: Collective bargaining talks kicked off Monday morning between Ford Motor Co. and Canada’s largest private sector union, which says it is anticipating the "most consequential round of auto bargaining" in its history.

items.title: Mbappe's 2 goals help France cruise past Iraq in match prolonged by 2-hour weather delay
items.summary_auto: Kylian Mbappe scored twice to move into a tie for second in career World Cup goals with 16, and France played through the tournament's first rain delay to beat Iraq 3-0 on Monday and advance to the knockout stage.

items.title: Fact check: Is this photo of the Ford Fest crowd edited?
items.summary_auto: Ontario Premier Doug Ford’s annual Ford Fest took place in Toronto on Friday evening. But does a photo shared by officials in his government appear to be edited to make protesters look like supporters?

items.title: Why the World Cup final halftime show is controversial
items.summary_auto: The 2026 FIFA World Cup final will feature a halftime show for the first time. It has ruffled the feathers of some soccer fanatics. Here's why.

items.title: Mexico, Canada have formed a common front on CUSMA talks, says Sheinbaum
items.summary_auto: Mexico has formed a common front with Canada during ongoing talks around the future of the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA), says Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum.

items.title: Toys 'R' Us Canada gets court approval to split business among 3 buyers
items.summary_auto: One of the deals includes the rights to the Toys "R" Us and Babies "R" Us Canada names and logos. Another deal includes assets like inventory, leases and equipment, and the final transaction is for a lease at Vaughan Mills mall.

items.title: Olivia Rodrigo announces all-women music festival featuring Chappell Roan, Sarah McLachlan, Stevie Nicks
items.summary_auto: Nearly 30 years after Sarah McLachlan launched what would become the massively successful all-women touring festival, Lilith Fair, Rodrigo is starting a festival of her own. It's called Daisy Chain Fields, and it features an all-women lineup.

items.title: Human rights museum board member resigns over 'one-sided' exhibit on displaced Palestinians
items.summary_auto: A trustee for the Canadian Museum for Human Rights says he has resigned from the Winnipeg facility's board over an upcoming exhibit about displaced Palestinians.

items.title: Halifax auditor general asks police to investigate mayor's expenses
items.summary_auto: The auditor general found four transactions from the mayor's office were non-compliant with city policy and has referred the matter to the RCMP.

items.title: Trump continues to allege D.C. reflecting pool was vandalized, as ex-Olympian denies doing damage
items.summary_auto: The Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool on Washington's National Mall is set to be drained again for repairs after algae and peeling paint appeared just weeks ​after a $14.7-million US renovation, while U.S President Donald Trump threatened ​prison time for anyone caught damaging the pool.

items.title: Ontario has unveiled mandatory changes for schools, starting this fall. Here's what you need to know
items.summary_auto: Mandatory changes are coming to schools across Ontario starting in September, from attendance, participation and exams counting towards secondary school students' final grades to a digital learning platform being implemented at all school boards.

items.title: Competition Bureau looking deeper into how Sobeys can control who sells food in your area
items.summary_auto: The Competition Bureau has obtained court orders requiring the parent company of Sobeys to produce documents and testimony for its investigation into property controls, which are agreements that can limit the places where a new grocery store can open.

items.title: Uber board sued over alleged failure to address sexual abuse by drivers
items.summary_auto: A group of shareholders accused Uber Technologies management and directors of ignoring internal and external warnings about Uber's alleged failure ​to address sexual abuse by drivers. A spokesperson for Uber says the lawsuit ignores important facts.

items.title: Tomatoes cost how much now? Why inflation has come for Canada's produce section
items.summary_auto: Tomato prices rose 45.2 per cent year-over-year in May, according to new inflation numbers released Monday. That's by far the highest price increase since May last year for any grocery item measured in the Consumer Price Index.

items.title: Lionel Messi becomes all-time World Cup leading goalscorer after Argentina's 2-0 win over Austria
items.summary_auto: Lionel Messi set a World Cup record with his 17th and 18th goals, and defending champion Argentina advanced to the knockout stage with a 2-0 victory over Austria on Monday in Arlington, Texas.

items.title: Ottawa reverses order to surrender some 'lost Canadian' citizenship certificates
items.summary_auto: Just one week after some "lost Canadians" were told to surrender their new citizenship certificates, a few received letters over the weekend confirming their citizenship claims are valid once again.
